context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on this 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class 😅. I’m not sure how to answer whether “development” still means anything today or how to connect that to the global crisis.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Before we tackle whether 'development' still has meaning today — what does the word mean to *you*, off the top of your head? Don't overthink it, just give me your instinctive answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and needs to be oriented toward the core questions without giving them any content or direction. I should use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about what 'development' means to them before diving into the prompt's complexity. Minimal scaffolding is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is like, improving people’s lives? But idk if that just means economic growth or also rights, health, education, all that.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're sensing — between economic growth and the broader stuff like rights and health — is actually at the heart of the first question in the prompt. So let me ask you this: do you think scholars, governments, and international organizations all agree on which of those definitions is the right one, or do you notice any disagreements when you look at the world around you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a genuinely good starting intuition that touches on the core tension in development theory (GDP-focused vs. multidimensional conceptions). I should validate the thinking briefly and push them to deepen it with a Socratic question that moves toward the assignment's first sub-question: does development have an agreed-upon core meaning today?

exchanges.student: yeah def disagreements. some act like GDP growth = development, but others care more abt inequality, capabilities, sustainability, or political freedom.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation. So here's the key question the prompt is pushing you toward: does the fact that all those different definitions exist mean the word 'development' has become meaningless — or could there still be some common core underneath all of them? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying real theoretical camps (growth-focused, capabilities, sustainability, rights-based) without necessarily knowing their names or deeper content. This is good progress. I should push them to draw a conclusion from this observation that directly addresses the prompt's question — does this disagreement mean development is 'meaningless'? This moves the conversation toward the core argument they'll need to make in their paper.

exchanges.student: I think there’s still some core, tbh. Like maybe not one perfect definition, but some shared idea of expanding well-being and people’s ability to live decently.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable position to anchor your argument on. Now the prompt asks you to go further — if that's your core definition, which countries or communities would you point to as *success stories* by that standard, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is forming a defensible position that could anchor their reflection piece. This is good progress. Rather than dwelling further on this point, I should push them forward toward the second sub-question in the prompt: which countries or communities would be role models for development success? This moves the conversation productively along the assignment's structure.

exchanges.student: Maybe Costa Rica? and maybe Kerala in India? I picked them kinda bc they seem strong on health/education, not just income, but I’m not 100% sure.
exchanges.tutor: Interesting choices. What specifically about Costa Rica or Kerala makes them fit *your* definition of development — the one about expanding well-being and living decently? Try to be concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has picked two commonly cited examples in development literature that fit their argument well. Rather than confirming or correcting their choices, I should push them to articulate *why* these examples work according to their own definition, which strengthens their argumentative writing. This also prepares them for the third sub-question about the global crisis.

exchanges.student: I guess Costa Rica has good health and education outcomes, and Kerala too, plus social development without being super rich. Is that too vague lol?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track, but 'good outcomes' is a bit loose. What *specific* outcomes would you point to, and how do those directly illustrate your core definition? Once you can tighten that, we should move to the global crisis part of the prompt — how are you thinking about that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being self-critical but is actually on the right track. Rather than reassuring them, I should push them to sharpen the connection between their examples and their definition, and then move them toward the third sub-question about the global crisis, since we're about halfway through our planned exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking life expectancy, literacy, maybe public health access? For the crisis part, idk if I should say too much market bc of deregulation, or too little good government/regulation.
exchanges.tutor: Those concrete indicators work much better. Now on the crisis — the prompt is asking you to take a stance. What's *your* argument? Too much market, too little government, or some combination? What evidence or reasoning would you use to back that up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress on the examples and is now engaging with the third sub-question. Rather than resolving the 'too much market vs. too little government' tension for them, I should push them to take a position and reason through it, since the prompt explicitly asks them to argue one way or the other.

exchanges.student: I’m leaning some combo: too much faith in markets at the global scale, and too little effective/regulatory government at both national and global levels. I’m just not sure how to phrase that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: I can't help with phrasing — that's your job as the writer. But focus on the logic first: you mention *scale* (national vs. global) which is actually a key part of the prompt. How does scale change the argument? Why does it matter *where* the regulatory failure happens?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear position and is now shifting toward asking me to help them with phrasing/writing, which I must refuse per the rules. I should redirect them to focus on the argument's logic rather than the language, and also remind them that the prompt asks about *scale* specifically, which they've touched on but could develop further. We're nearing the end of our planned exchanges so I should start pushing toward integration.

exchanges.student: I think scale matters bc markets operate across borders, but regulation is often weaker or stuck at the nation-state level. So a crisis can be global, while the tools to control it are fragmented.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid insight that ties together the crisis question well. You now have the building blocks of your reflection: a definition of development, role model examples with concrete indicators, and an argument about markets, governance, and scale. It's time to start writing. Give it a first shot and come back if you get stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a strong, coherent argument across all three sub-questions of the prompt. We're close to the end of our planned exchanges. It's time to push the student toward integration — they should now attempt to write their reflection, connecting their definition of development, their role model examples, and their crisis argument into a coherent draft. This is a natural integration moment.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, I think my main claim is clearer now. Before I draft, can u quickly sanity-check if this structure makes sense: define the core of development, argue success is multidimensional with Costa Rica/Kerala, then connect the crisis to overreliance on markets plus weak regulation across scales?
exchanges.tutor: That structure sounds coherent and addresses all three parts of the prompt. Now stop planning and start writing — the draft will reveal where the real gaps are. Go for it!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for validation of their structure, which is reasonable and doesn't require me to write anything for them. I can give a brief general evaluation without grading, then push them to start drafting since we've reached the end of our planned exchanges and integration is the priority now.
